items.title: Low-intensity focused ultrasound to human amygdala reveals a causal role in ambiguous emotion processing and alters local and network activity
items.author: Johannes Algermissen, Miruna Rascu, Lilian A. Weber, Tim den Boer, Eleanor Martin, Bradley Treeby, Michael D. Gray, Robin O. Cleveland, Marco K. Wittmann, William T. Clarke, Elsa Fouragnan, Matthew F.S. Rushworth, Miriam C. Klein-Flügge
items.content: <article>Algermissen et al. show that non-invasive transcranial focused ultrasound stimulation applied offline can modulate human amygdala function in healthy volunteers. Following bilateral stimulation, people are more likely to approach perceptually ambiguous neutral faces. These effects are specific to the stimulation site and are reflected in changes in resting-state connectivity and metabolite concentrations.</article>

items.title: Ennio Pannese (1928–2025)
items.author: Menachem Hanani, Valeria Cavalli
items.content: <article>Ennio Pannese, professor at the University of Milan’s Faculty of Medicine and a distinguished figure in the field of histology and neuroscience, passed away on November 7, 2025. His groundbreaking research, commitment to education, and dedication to scientific advancement have left a profound legacy in the study of neurobiology.</article>

items.title: When breaking down helps you move forward: Pursuing complex goals
items.author: Zilu Liang, Miriam C. Klein-Flügge
items.content: <article>Achieving complex goals often requires breaking them down into simpler subgoals. In this issue of Neuron, Grossman et al. show that people structure their behavior hierarchically when pursuing goals, and they reveal candidate brain computations that support this planning.</article>

items.title: Feature-tuned excitation of somatostatin interneurons drives figure-ground segregation
items.author: Yuan E. Zhang, Huizhong Whit Tao
items.content: <article>Visual pop-out reflects feature-dependent surround modulation in primary visual cortex (V1). In this issue of Neuron, Hendricks et al. reveal that co-tuned pyramidal cells selectively engage somatostatin interneurons (SSTs) via like-to-like excitation, switching cortical circuits into a suppressed state that supports orientation-based figure-ground segmentation.</article>

items.title: Honeybee GABAA receptor structure informs new insecticide development
items.author: Alyssa H. Marinas, Ryan E. Hibbs
items.content: <article>GABAA receptors are critical in insect neurotransmission. How insecticides act on these receptors in pest insects and in beneficial insects like honeybees remains underexplored. Here, Labouré et al.1 reveal how insecticides modulate honeybee GABAA receptors, facilitating design of safer pesticides.</article>

items.title: Rethinking two-photon voltage imaging
items.author: Shuzhang Liu, Luxin Peng, Peng Zou
items.content: <article>In this issue of Neuron, Grimm et al. describe the Jarvis voltage indicator, which demonstrates that scanless parallel two-photon excitation enables robust, high-speed voltage imaging in vivo, overturning prior assumptions about the incompatibility of rhodopsin-based indicators with two-photon microscopy.</article>

items.title: A population approach to cortical GABAergic interneuron function
items.author: Johannes J. Letzkus, Henning Sprekeler, Harald Binder, Joschka Bödecker, Ilka Diester, Claudio Elgueta, Sabine Grosser, Matthias Haberl, Akos Kulik, Matthew Larkum, Christian Leibold, Christian Madry, Hannah Monyer, James F.A. Poulet, Jonas-Frederic Sauer, Jan Schmoranzer, Susanne Schreiber, Julia Veit, Silvia Viana da Silva, Andreas Vlachos, Imre Vida, Jörg R.P. Geiger, Marlene Bartos
items.content: <article>Inhibitory interneuron diversity is a central feature of cortical circuits. The IN-CODE consortium seeks to combine large-scale recordings of interneuron types with machine-learning tools to identify the role of their physiological features, connectivity motives, and cooperativity in cognitive functions.</article>
